title: Tea loaf
subtitle: Dried fruit soaked overnight in strong tea, baked in a loaf tin
excerpt: Currants, sultanas, and raisins soaked overnight in strong tea, then mixed with flour, brown sugar, and an egg and baked into a moist, dark loaf. No butter, no fat, no fuss.
sourceNotes: The tea loaf is a British baking tradition documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), Project Gutenberg, as a regional economy cake common to areas where dried fruit was a valued commodity. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes several boiled and soaked fruit cakes from which the modern tea loaf descends. The overnight soak in tea is the defining step, the tannins in the tea add colour and a slight bitterness that balances the sweetness of the fruit.

content.text: Tea loaf is one of the most forgiving things you can bake. The method is: soak fruit overnight in tea, then combine it with flour, sugar, and egg and bake. There is no creaming, no folding, no technique to master. The result is a dense, dark,
loaf that keeps for a week and improves from the second day.

The overnight soak is important: the dried fruit absorbs the tea and plumps up, and the tannins in the tea stain it a deep mahogany and add a slight bitterness that stops the loaf being cloying. Use a strong black tea, two teabags, brewed for four minutes and left to cool before pouring over the fruit.

Put {{currants}}, {{sultanas}}, {{raisins}}, and {{dark-brown-sugar}} into a large bowl. Brew 300 ml of strong black tea and pour it over the fruit while still warm. Stir to dissolve the sugar. Cover and leave to soak overnight at room temperature, at least eight hours.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan (180°C conventional). Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin with baking paper. Sift {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{mixed-spice}} into the soaked fruit and add {{eggs}}. Stir everything together until just combined; do not overmix. The batter will be thick and heavy with fruit. Scrape into the prepared tin and level the surface.

Bake for 65 to 70 minutes. Test with a skewer: it should come out clean with no wet batter attached. If the top is colouring too quickly, tent loosely with foil. An oven thermometer helps with rich fruit loaves, which can burn at the top while the centre remains wet if the oven runs hot. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then on a rack. Leave for at least one day before slicing.
Serve in thick slices with butter. The loaf keeps well for a week at room temperature in an airtight tin, and improves on the second and third day as the crumb settles.
Earl Grey tea in the soak gives a floral note. A splash of whisky added to the cooled tea is good in a more grown-up version.
The loaf is naturally dairy-free as written. It is also reasonably low in fat, since there is no butter; the moisture comes entirely from the soaked fruit and the egg.
